coredata.dc:title: Hyaluronic acid-functionalized gold nanoparticles as a cancer diagnostic probe for targeted bioimaging applications
coredata.dc:creator: Hejazi, Maryam
Arshadi, Sina
Amini, Mohammad
Baradaran, Behzad
Shahbazi-Derakhshi, Payam
Sameti, Pouriya
Soleymani, Jafar
Mokhtarzadeh, Ahad
Tavangar, Seyed Mohammad
coredata.dc:description: A nanomaterial-based bioimaging method was developed to detect the MCF-7 cancer cells using hyaluronic acid (HA) functionalized-gold nanoparticles/polyetherimide (PEI)-stabilized/rhodamine B (RhB) (AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA) materials. The fabricated materials have low-toxic nature with exceptional specific attachment to the CD44 receptor overexerted MCF-7-cancer cells. The toxicity test of the materials was evaluated by MTT assay with threshold toxicity concentration of 100 µg/mL. This finding was validated using DAPI staining assay showing no cell death during exposure to nanoparticles. The bioimaging of the AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA were evaluated by flow cytometry and fluorescence imaging techniques. Also, the cell differentiation of the AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA was also investigated by incubation with CD44-low expressed HFFF-2 as control group. The uptake percentage of the AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA nanoparticles for MCF-7 cells were 13.1%, 52.4% and 97.5% for 10 µg/mL, 20 µg/mL and 50 µg/mL after about 4 h of incubation, respectively. However, these values for HFFF-2 cells were around 0.88%, 16.1% and 33.9%, respectively, confirming selective manner of the probe towards CD44-overxeperssed cells. The produced nanoparticles displayed high physical stability with high quantum yield, qualifying the nanoparticles as a brilliant material for in vivo cancer diagnosis aims.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biomedical
Bioimaging
Cancer diagnostics
Fluorescent probe
Membrane receptor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: UV and fluorescence spectra of AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA
Effect of pH on the absorbance and fluorescence of the AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to acknowledge the financial supports from Tabriz University of Medical Sciences (Tabriz, Iran) with grant number of 68315.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FESEM and (b) AFM results of AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA.
Fluorescence bioimaging of uptake of the AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA at different incubation times in (a) MCF-7 and (b) HFFF-2 cell lines.
Flow cytometry of uptake of the AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA at different time (1–4 h) in MCF-7 and HFFF-2 cell lines.
Flow cytometry of uptake of the AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA at different concentrations (10, 20, and 50 µg/mL) in MCF-7 and HFFF-2 cell lines.
(A) Synthesis steps of AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA.and (B and C) bioimaging of MCF-7 cells.

head.ce:title: Hyaluronic acid-functionalized gold nanoparticles as a cancer diagnostic probe for targeted bioimaging applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Pharmaceutical Analysis Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran (J. Soleymani), Immunology Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran (A. Mokhtarzadeh), and Chronic Diseases Research Center, Endocrinology and Metabolism Population Sciences Institute, Tehran University of Medical Sciences, Tehran, Iran (S.M. Tavangar).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A nanomaterial-based bioimaging method was developed to detect the MCF-7 cancer cells using hyaluronic acid (HA) functionalized-gold nanoparticles/polyetherimide (PEI)-stabilized/rhodamine B (RhB) (AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA) materials. The fabricated materials have low-toxic nature with exceptional specific attachment to the CD44 receptor overexerted MCF-7-cancer cells. The toxicity test of the materials was evaluated by MTT assay with threshold toxicity concentration of 100 µg/mL. This finding was validated using DAPI staining assay showing no cell death during exposure to nanoparticles. The bioimaging of the AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA were evaluated by flow cytometry and fluorescence imaging techniques. Also, the cell differentiation of the AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA was also investigated by incubation with CD44-low expressed HFFF-2 as control group. The uptake percentage of the AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA nanoparticles for MCF-7 cells were 13.1%, 52.4% and 97.5% for 10 µg/mL, 20 µg/mL and 50 µg/mL after about 4 h of incubation, respectively. However, these values for HFFF-2 cells were around 0.88%, 16.1% and 33.9%, respectively, confirming selective manner of the probe towards CD44-overxeperssed cells. The produced nanoparticles displayed high physical stability with high quantum yield, qualifying the nanoparticles as a brilliant material for in vivo cancer diagnosis aims.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: One of the main health problems with a high risk level that humans are dealing with is cancer, which according to the reports of the American Cancer Society, nearly half of the 19.3 million people with this disease, means about 10 million people, have not survived this disease in 2020. The incidence rate of this disease is increasing fast so that in 2040 we may face with 28.4 million cases which is 47% more than 2020 [1,2]. Tumor metastasis step is the critical step in cancer disease in which a metastatic tumor forms which their cells are very similar to the primary cells. Various methods with different basics were utilized for the diagnosis of cancer, however, imaging is one of the most appropriate methods in clinical uses [3–5].

Currently, magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), ultrasound (US), computed tomography (CT), etc. are employing for locating of solid tumors as the cancer diagnostic techniques [6,7]. These techniques propose drawbacks including expensive and laborious procedures with biased results in which there is a need for a much expertized person to operate and interpret the obtained results. Today, the application of nanotechnology is brought more efficient and simple bioimaging approaches for the exact locating of solid tumors.

An increasing interest has been drawn to the spectroscopic imaging-based cancer diagnostic techniques [8–12]. In this regard, highly fluorescence materials play the main role due to their advantageous features of high quantum yield (QY), large stocks shift, bright fluorescence emission, etc. [13–17]. However, the materials with excitation and emission wavelengths in the visible and near-infrared (NIR) ranges are of great importance especially in the in vivo bioimaging media because of their low-invasive nature, making negligible damage to the live organisms [18]. Fluorescence-based bioimaging offers quantitative and sensitive approaches with simple and cheap operation process [19–21]. However, targeted bioimaging using antibody/antigen, aptamer, receptors, etc. proposes enhanced selectivity of the applied bioimaging approaches [22–24]. Different types of fluorescent materials has been utilized for bioimaging of cancer cells. For example, aptamer-functionalized graphene quantum dots (A549 cells) [25], folic acid (FA)-functionalized carbon nanodots (CDs) (HeLa cells) [26], concanavalin A-conjugated AuNPs/SiQDs-Con A (MCF-7 cells) [27], etc. were utilized as fluorescence-based bioimaging nanomaterials. The main criteria for the selection of the nanomaterials are included biocompatibility, QY, spectrum brightness, low energy excitation and emission, thermal stability, shelf life, leaching in biological media, etc.

CD44, known as a non-kinase transmembrane glycoprotein, plays a role in various pathways with results such as increased cell survival, cell proliferation and increased cell motility. The data obtained from different studies indicated that the role of CD44 in the regulation of epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition can lead to metastasis in case of overexpression in cancer cells. The binding of cytoskeletal elements or adapter proteins that occurs following the structural changes of CD44 that occur as a result of the binding of its main ligand, hyaluronic acid (HA), leads to the promotion of cell proliferation, invasion and migration in cancer cells [28]. CD44 bioreceptor was utilized as a specific membrane protein for drug delivery and imaging purposes [29–32]. HA-functionalized agents can specifically attach to the CD44-overexpressed cancer cells using HA/CD44 interaction with high formation constant.

In the current study, the AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA were employed as a cell targeting material for bioimaging MCF-7 tumoral cells (Scheme 1 ). The cell uptake of the nanomaterials was evaluated through different techniques where fluorescence microscopy and flow cytometry techniques were applied for qualitative and quantitative evaluation of the cell internalization. HFFF-2 as folate negative cell was regarded as a control group to assay the specificity of the AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA nanoparticles. This bioimaging method was effectively utilized for recognition of FA-positive MCF-7 cells and also would be applied as a promising cancer diagnostic platform for reliable differentiation of other FA-overexpressing cells from normal FA-low-expressing ones.

HA, N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, 98.0%), rhodamine B (RhB, 98%),1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC, 98.0%), and 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) were provided from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). Hydrochloric acid (HCl), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), and dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO) were purchased from Merck (Germany). Gold (III) chloride trihydrate (HAuCl4⋅3H2O, 99.9%), phosphate buffer, trisaminomethane hydrochloride (Tris-HCl) buffer solids were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (Germany). Triton X-100 and 4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI) were purchased from Sigma (Germany). Fetal bovine serum (FBS), Roswell Park Memorial Institute (RPMI) 1640 culture media, penicillin/streptomycin, and trypsin-EDTA solution (25%) were purchased from Gibco Co. (UK). The source of supply of the cell lines was from National Cell Bank of Iran (NCBI) at Pasteur institute (Tehran, Iran).

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) of the materials was recorded using a Bruker FTIR spectrometer (Massachusetts, USA). Fluorescence spectra of the AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA nanoparticles were measured using a JASCO FP 750 fluorimeter (Tokyo, Japan). Fluorescence bioimages were captured using a microscope of Olympus Corporation (BX64 model, Tokyo, Japan). However surface morphology and energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis of the nanoparticles were determined using a field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, FEG-SEM MIRA3 TESCAN model, Brno, Czech Republic) technique. Size distribution and surface charge of the nanoparticles were determined by a size analyzer instrument (Malvern, UK). Flow cytometry of the treated cells was recorded by a BD Biosciences instrument (CA, USA). The cell toxicity of the nanomaterials was measured by the Awareness Technology plate reader (Florida, USA). Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) analysis was conducted through the Nanosurf mobile S (Liestal, Switzerland).
For further information, please see supplementary file (SI).

MCF-7 and HFFF-2 cell lines were firstly thawed and cultured in the RPMI 1640 medium (enriched with FBS (10%) and penicillin/streptomycin (1%)) and incubated at 37° C for growing of the cells. The cells should be re-cultured for two or three times to be ready for the next tests.

The toxicity of the AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA nanoparticles towards MCF-7 and HFFF-2 cell lines was measured by MTT assay. About 10,000 cells were added into each well a 96-well cell culture plate and incubated for a given time. HA-functionalized gold nanocomposite with different concentrations (1 to 100 mg/L) were added to the wells and stored in cell culture oven (37 °C and 5% CO2) for the desired incubation times. MTT solution (10 µL of 6 mg/mL) was added to each well and incubated about 4 h. The wells liquids were replaced by 200 µL DMSO and incubated for about 0.5 h. A microplate reader was used to recode the absorbance of the well at 570 nm.

To further illustrate the cytotoxic effects of HA-functionalized gold nanoparticles on MCF-7 and HFFF-2 cells, apoptosis induction was evaluated using DAPI staining. In brief, the cells at a density of 1 × 103 were seeded into 96-well plates and incubated for about 24 h. The cells were treated with different amounts of HA-functionalized gold nanoparticles i.e. 10, 50 and 100 mg/L, and incubated for 24 h. The cells were washed with buffer and fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde for 1 h and permeabilized through incubation with 0.1% of Triton X-100 solution for 1 h. The cells rewashed with buffer solution and then stained by 10 μg/mL DAPI solution (Sigma, Germany) for 15 min. Finally, the DAPI solution was replaced with PBS and the cells were subjected to a cell imaging system (Cytation 5 Multi‐Mode Reader, BioTek, USA) to visually evaluate DNA fragmentation in treatment groups.

In order to evaluate the cell internalization of HA-functionalized gold nanoparticles composite, the MCF-7 cell lines were treated with the materials at the appropriate concentration and times. To do this, 5 × 105 of the MCF-7 cells were seeded into the 6-well plates [33]. After attachment of the cells to the plate surface, the wells were treated with the AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA. The cell uptake of the AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA was measured by both fluorescence microscopy and flow cytometry to assess the qualitative and quantitative amount of the internalization respectively.

All data obtained through this study were reported as mean ± standard error. Flow cytometry data points were analyzed using FlowJo software version 10. GraphPad Prism software version 8 was employed to perform the statistical analysis. To examine the statistical significance between two groups and more than two groups, T-test and one-way analysis of variance were used, respectively. We considered P values of < 0.05 statistically significant.

The FESEM images of AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA nanoparticles are presented in Fig. 1 (a). These nanoparticles are in a rod-like structure with mean sizes around 100 nm. Although, AuNPs are in spherical shape as stabilized by sodium citrate, addition of the PEI/RhB-HA leads to the production of rod-like structure AuNPs [34]. The obtained size is near to the hydrated DLS size of AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA with a diameter of about 125 nm (Fig. S1(a)). EDX results of AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA materials confirmed the existence of O, N, C and Au elements on their structure (Fig. S2 and Table S1). The zeta potential of AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA nanomaterials was around −11.8 mV equaling to the average of positive zeta potential of PEI and negative zeta potential of RhB (Fig. S1(b)) [35,36]. Fig. 1(b) displays a characteristic AFM image of an AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA structure which also directs the rod-shaped and single crystal morphology with a diameter of about 100 nm. Also, the height of nanoparticles was between 50 and 100 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV and fluorescence spectra of AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA
ce:section.ce:para: UV spectrum of RhB, AuNPs/PEI/RhB and AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA nanomaterial are presented in Fig. S4(a). RhB possesses three main peaks at 257 nm, 353 nm, and 553 nm as the characteristic absorption bands of RhB. The n-π* electron transition is responsible for the absorption peak at 257 nm while π-π* transition is observed as a peak at 553 nm. There was no obvious change in the characteristic peaks of RhB after conjugating to the AuNPs/PEI/RhB and AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA nanoparticles. However, a sharp decreasing on the absorbance of RhB is observed after anchoring to AuNPs. The reduction in the absorbance of RhB was further continued after bonding to the AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA. These results proved that the attachment of HA and RhB on AuNPs surface. AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA showed two absorption bonds at 257 nm and 553 nm where 553 nm absorption bond is resulted by π-π* electron transitions of the anchored RhB molecules. The π-π* electron transitions is responsible for the AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA photoluminescence emission (Fig. S4(b)). Typical excitation and photoluminescence spectra of the AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA are shown in Fig. S5.

Influence of several excitation wavelengths on the photoluminescence of AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA was also studied. The results revealed that the luminescence intensity of the AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA increased by enhancing the excitation wavelength from 450 to 550 nm. However, the emission wavelength of AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA nanoparticles were remained constant, approving the Kasha’s rule (Fig. S6). In this study, 550 nm was selected as the optimized excitation wavelengths for further tests.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH on the absorbance and fluorescence of the AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. S7 shows the influence of pH on the absorption and fluorescent peak intensity and location of AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA. The absorbance of AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA is enhanced by increasing pH to pH 4.5 and then remained constant pHs between 5.5 and 10.5. Also, peak maxima were altered from 557 nm to 553 nm of pH 2.5 and 10.5, respectively. Also, fluorescence emission of AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA was largely affected by pH where it had an increasing trend from 2.5 to 4.5 and was constant in the pH range of 4.5–8.5. However, the fluorescence emission of AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA was decreased from pH of 8.5 to 10.5. The AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA possesses many functional groups like hydroxyl, amine, carboxyl, amid, etc. which severely are affected by the environment condition. However, the fluorescence intensity of the AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA was more affected than peak wavelength due to the coverage of the RhB layer by HA.

Relative QY of AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA was determined using RhB as the reference fluorescent molecule [37] with a QY of 30 % in aqueous solution. To calculate QY, three concentrations of AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA and RhB were prepared to reach absorbances below 0.1. Then, the fluorescence emissions were recorded at the concentrations. Next, photoluminescence vs. absorbance plots of AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA and RhB were provided. QY of the nanoparticle could be calculated by the following equation:
Q Y
S
=
Q Y
R
A R
A S
F S
F R
η S
η R
2
In this equation, ‘‘S” and “R” refer to the AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA and RhB, respectively. F, A, and η are the fluorescence spectrum area, absorbance and refractive index, respectively. The QY of AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA were calculated around 29.2 % which is near to the QY of RhB. Fig. S8 provide the QY calculation process.

The stability of nanoparticles strongly influences their synthesis, storage and possible applications. Various parameters such as ionic strength, exposure time, fluorescence recoding time, storage time and temperature were investigated in the best conditions. The influence of ionic strength is regarded as an important factor due to high concentrations of ions in biological media. The effect of NaCl and KCl was studied on the fluorescence intensity (stability) of AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA (Fig. S9(a)). The addition of NaCl and KCl with high electrolyte capacities can regulate the intermolecular forces to enhance the fluorescence of the AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA. However, in higher concentrations of the salts, it may led to the leaching of AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA.

The obtained results revealed that AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA nanocomposite was stable toward UV irradiations and only after one hour, significant decrease on the fluorescence emission was observed. These results approved favorable strength of the AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA towards photobleaching (Fig. S9(b)). The effect of incubation time was also tested which the results suggested that the fluorescence intensity of AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA was stable at the first 10 min of incubation without significant alteration on the fluorescence emission (Fig. S9(c)). The effect of storing stability was tested which the obtained results confirmed the acceptable stability in the first two day at room temperature (Fig. S9(d)). Upon increasing the incubation temperature, the fluorescence intensity logically decreased because of increased intermolecular interactions and non-radiation relaxations (Fig. S9 (e)).

The cell toxicity of AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA was checked against MCF-7 cancer cells and HFFF-2 human normal cells using MTT assay. The cytotoxicity of AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA was determined at 1, 5, 10, 50, 100 µg/mL concentrations through 24 h of incubation (Fig. S10). The results shown that the cell viability of MCF-7 and HFFF-2 cell lines was not changed significantly through exposure of cells to different concentrations of AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA compared to the control cells. Then, the results approved that the MCF-7 and HFFF-2 cell survival rate was not affected by AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA at the concentrations and the produced nanoparticles could be further considered for use in the in vivo studies.

Moreover, to confirm that AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA has no cytotoxic and apoptotic effects on human cells, DAPI staining assay was carried out. As shown in Fig. S11, the exposure of MCF-7 and HFFF-2 cells to different concentrations of AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA nanoparticles, including 10, 50 and 100 µg/mL didn’t lead to chromatin fragmentation and the nuclei of treated cells were intact just like those of control cells.

CD44 receptor targeting ability of AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA was evaluated by comparison of the internalization value between MCF-7 (as CD44 overexpressing cells) and HFFF-2 (as CD44 low-expressing cells). Fig. 2 exhibits the fluorescence bioimages of MCF-7 cancer cells and HFFF-2 cells after treatment with the AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA nanocomposite for 2 and 4 h. As see, the morphology of the MCF-7 and HFFF-2 cells was almost unchanged after about 4 h of incubation with the nanomaterials. Furthermore, the cell attached form of AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA were presented in red color. When AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA nanoparticles are conjugated into CD44-overexpressing MCF-7 cells via HA and CD44 interactions, the place of the cells is distinguished by a simple fluorescence microscope.

The AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA emitted red fluorescence emission after anchoring to the surface on the CD44-overexpressed cells. The internalization amount of AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA to the MCF-7 cells is improved by increasing the incubation time. Red fluorescence emission was detected in the surface membrane and cytoplasm nor the nucleus of the cells, demonstrating that the AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA are stay in membrane of the cells and slowly penetrate into the cytoplasm of MCF-7 cells rather than nucleus. This phenomenon is in accordance with the hypothesis of the presence of the CD44 receptors on the surface membrane of MCF-7 cells. The cell uptake of the AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA was reached at the highest level after 4 h of incubation, with an increasing trends from the start time. In addition, the uptake percentage was proportionate to the concertation of the AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA. The high level of AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA can result to the brighter images which is a result of the enhanced number of CD44 and HA interactions. The fluorescence bioimages presents qualitative levels of uptake of AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA to the MCF-7 cells. However, to provide quantitative results, the flow cytometry technique is utilized to measure internalization level of the AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA (Fig. 3 ).

Fig. 4 displays the results of the treatment of the MCF-7 cancer cells with different concentrations of the AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA nanoparticles. In this study, the MCF-7 cells were incubated with 10, 20 and 50 µg/mL of the AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA nanoparticles. Results showed that at the higher concentrations (>50 µg/mL), the fluorescence emission intensity was stayed constant. Also, it is obviously concluded the high level of uptake of the AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA with CD44 overexpressing MCF-7 cells compared to HFFF-2 cells with lower expression levels of CD44.

The targeting ability of the AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA nanoparticles was investigated by fluorescence microscopy following treatment of HFFF-2 cells with the nanoparticles [38]. Obtained bioimaging results proposed that the AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA failed to attach to the HFFF-2 cells due to low surface expression of CD44 receptors. The high affinity of AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA to CD44 of MCF-7 cell membrane is generally caused by high strength of CD44/HA bond, resulting to the targeted bioimaging of the CD44 overexpressed cells (Figs. 2, 3 and 4). Also, the specificity of the AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA was also determined in an inhibiting way. Fig. S12 proves the specificity of the AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA towards the MCF-7 cancer cells. In this method, the CD44 receptors are occupied by adding an excess amount of HA molecules. After saturation of CD44 receptors with the HA, uptake of the AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA is decreased dramatically, confirming the specific manner of the nanoparticles in the targeting of CD44 overexpressed cells.

A novel HA-functionalized Au-based materials i.e. AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA was produced for targeting of MCF-7 cancer cells via HA/CD44 interactions. The non-toxic effect of AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA nanocomposite on viability MCF-7 breast cancer cells and HFFF-2 as normal cells was approved using MTT assay and DAPI staining, estimating its biocompatible concentrations ranging from 10 to 100 µg/L. The obtained results also implied that AuNPs/PEI/RhB-HA nanoparticles can uptake to the breast cancer cells using CD44 surface bioreceptors with exceptional selectivity. The obtained results were quantitatively confirmed by flow cytometry technique. The synthesized nanocomposite is a favorable material from point of view of cell viability and targeting and emits a bright fluorescence emission at the visible range which could be recommended for the in vivo cancer diagnostic bioimaging aims.

Maryam Hejazi: Investigation, Methodology, Validation, Writing – original draft. Sina Arshadi: Investigation, Methodology, Validation. Mohammad Amini: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Behzad Baradaran: Project administration, Resources, Writing – review & editing. Payam Shahbazi-Derakhshi: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Pouriya Sameti: Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Jafar Soleymani: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Ahad Mokhtarzadeh: Investigation, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Seyed Mohammad Tavangar: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge the financial supports from Tabriz University of Medical Sciences (Tabriz, Iran) with grant number of 68315.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2023.108953.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The progress and perspective of nanoparticle-enabled tumor metastasis treatment

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Zhang, F. Wang, C. Hu, Y. Zhou, H. Gao, J. Hu, The progress and perspective of nanoparticle-enabled tumor metastasis treatment, Acta Pharm. Sin. B. 10 (2020) 2037–2053. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsb.2020.07.013.
R.L. Siegel, K.D. Miller, A. Jemal, Cancer statistics, 2020, CA. Cancer J. Clin. 70 (2020) 7–30. https://doi.org/10.3322/caac.21590.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances in electrochemical and electrochemiluminescence based determination of the activity of caspase-3

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Development of a reliable microRNA based electrochemical genosensor for monitoring of miR-146a, as key regulatory agent of neurodegenerative disease

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Khalilzadeh, M. Rashidi, A. Soleimanian, H. Tajalli, G.S. Kanberoglu, B. Baradaran, M.-R. Rashidi, Development of a reliable microRNA based electrochemical genosensor for monitoring of miR-146a, as key regulatory agent of neurodegenerative disease, Int. J. Biol. Macromol. 134 (2019) 695–703. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2019.05.061.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nanoparticles in cancer treatment: opportunities and obstacles
